quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Wave equation"?

options.text: ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ_(Z-1) Y + e⁺ + ν; proton → neutron + positron + neutrino; occurs in proton-rich nuclei
v = fλ; wave speed equals frequency times wavelength; speed depends on the medium, not the wave itself
r = mv/(qB); radius of circular path depends on mass, speed, and charge-to-field ratio; the basis for particle accelerators
P + ½ρv² + ρgy = constant; higher velocity means lower pressure; the basis for lift, venturi effects, and flow measurement

quiz_questions.explanation: "Wave equation" is defined as: v = fλ; wave speed equals frequency times wavelength; speed depends on the medium, not the wave itself. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Transverse wave"?

options.text: P + ½ρv² + ρgy = constant; higher velocity means lower pressure; the basis for lift, venturi effects, and flow measurement
Oscillation is perpendicular to the direction of propagation; examples: light waves, waves on a string, S-waves
ΣΔV = 0 around any closed loop; based on conservation of energy; EMF gains = resistive drops
Many slits produce very sharp bright fringes at d sin θ = mλ; used for precise wavelength measurement (spectroscopy)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Transverse wave" is defined as: Oscillation is perpendicular to the direction of propagation; examples: light waves, waves on a string, S-waves. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Longitudinal wave"?

options.text: Oscillation is parallel to the direction of propagation; compressions and rarefactions; examples: sound waves, P-waves
Point fingers in direction of v (or I), curl toward B; thumb points in force direction for positive charge
1/f = (n-1)(1/R₁ - 1/R₂); relates focal length to the lens material (n) and surface curvatures (R₁, R₂)
1/λ = R_H(1/n_f² - 1/n_i²); predicts hydrogen spectral line wavelengths; R_H ≈ 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹

quiz_questions.explanation: "Longitudinal wave" is defined as: Oscillation is parallel to the direction of propagation; compressions and rarefactions; examples: sound waves, P-waves. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Superposition principle"?

options.text: ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ_(Z-1) Y + e⁺ + ν; proton → neutron + positron + neutrino; occurs in proton-rich nuclei
Δm = (Z·m_p + N·m_n) - m_nucleus; the 'missing mass' converted to binding energy; Δm > 0 for all stable nuclei
Gas pressure results from molecular collisions with container walls; temperature measures average translational kinetic energy: KE_avg = (3/2)kT
When two waves meet, the resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements; leads to interference patterns

quiz_questions.explanation: "Superposition principle" is defined as: When two waves meet, the resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements; leads to interference patterns. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Standing waves"?

options.text: f < 0; causes parallel light to diverge as if from a virtual focal point; always produces virtual, upright, reduced images
Formed by superposition of two identical waves traveling in opposite directions; nodes (zero displacement) and antinodes (maximum displacement)
V = U/q = kQ/r for a point charge; scalar quantity measured in volts (V = J/C)
Peaks at iron-56; lighter nuclei release energy by fusion; heavier nuclei release energy by fission

quiz_questions.explanation: "Standing waves" is defined as: Formed by superposition of two identical waves traveling in opposite directions; nodes (zero displacement) and antinodes (maximum displacement). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "v = fλ; wave speed equals frequency times wavelength; speed depends on the medium, not the wave itself"?

options.text: Malus's law
Wave equation
Emission spectrum
Rayleigh criterion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Wave equation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Oscillation is perpendicular to the direction of propagation; examples: light waves, waves on a string, S-waves"?

options.text: Lensmaker's equation
Bohr model of hydrogen
Reversible vs. irreversible
Transverse wave

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Transverse wave".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Oscillation is parallel to the direction of propagation; compressions and rarefactions; examples: sound waves, P-waves"?

options.text: Chain reaction
Quantum tunneling
Longitudinal wave
Pair production

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Longitudinal wave".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "When two waves meet, the resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements; leads to interference patterns"?

options.text: Superposition principle
Continuity equation
Dielectric constant (κ)
Magnetic flux

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Superposition principle".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Formed by superposition of two identical waves traveling in opposite directions; nodes (zero displacement) and antinodes (maximum displacement)"?

options.text: Charge conservation
Standing waves
Magnetic flux
Thin-film interference

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Standing waves".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Waves is accurate?

options.text: Both release energy because the products have higher binding energy per nucleon.
Converging (convex) lenses are thicker in the middle and bring parallel rays to a focus; diverging (concave) lenses are thinner in the middle and spread rays apart.
An absorption spectrum shows dark lines at the same wavelengths when continuous light passes through a cool gas—the gas absorbs photons matching its transition energies.
Waves are disturbances that transfer energy without transferring matter.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Waves as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Waves?

options.text: Thin-film interference involves path length differences in reflected light: constructive or destructive depending on the film thickness, wavelength, and indices of refraction.
Lenses use refraction to form images.
Pressure is defined as force per unit area: P = F/A.
Mechanical waves (sound, water, seismic) require a medium; electromagnetic waves (light, radio) do not.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Waves as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Waves?

options.text: Light as a Wave examines the wave nature of light in detail.
The intensity and spectrum of thermal radiation depend on temperature (Wien's law: λ_max = b/T; Stefan-Boltzmann: P = σAT⁴).
Key properties: wavelength (λ), frequency (f), amplitude (A), and wave speed (v = fλ).
Inserting a dielectric (insulating material) between the plates increases capacitance by a factor κ: C = κε₀A/d.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Waves as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Waves?

options.text: Transverse waves oscillate perpendicular to propagation (light, string waves); longitudinal waves oscillate parallel (sound).
Brewster's angle: reflected light is completely polarized when tan θ_B = n₂/n₁.
The Rydberg formula for hydrogen: 1/λ = R_H(1/n_f² - 1/n_i²), where R_H = 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹.
The modern quantum model replaces orbits with probability clouds (orbitals) described by wave functions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Waves as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Waves?

options.text: Superposition: when waves overlap, their displacements add algebraically, producing constructive or destructive interference.
Wave Properties and Behavior explores the fundamental characteristics common to all waves.
Magnetic Properties of Materials classifies materials by their response to external magnetic fields.
The intensity and spectrum of thermal radiation depend on temperature (Wien's law: λ_max = b/T; Stefan-Boltzmann: P = σAT⁴).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Waves as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Waves?

options.text: Seismology: P-waves (longitudinal) and S-waves (transverse) travel through Earth at different speeds, allowing scientists to locate earthquake epicenters and map Earth's interior structure
Xerox copiers use electrostatic attraction: a charged drum attracts toner particles to form an image, then heat fuses the toner to paper
Smoke detectors: americium-241 emits alpha particles that ionize air, creating a current; smoke disrupts the current, triggering the alarm
Electron microscopes achieve resolutions of ~0.1 nm by using electron 'lenses' (magnetic fields) instead of optical lenses—visualizing individual atoms

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Waves.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Waves?

options.text: Electron diffraction microscopy: electrons with de Broglie wavelength ~0.01 nm reveal atomic-scale structures in crystals—confirming matter's wave nature
PET scans detect positron annihilation: the 1.022 MeV of mass-energy from an electron-positron pair converts to two 0.511 MeV gamma rays traveling in opposite directions
Musical instruments: standing waves on strings and in air columns produce specific frequencies (harmonics); the length, tension, and density determine the pitch
Energy storage: supercapacitors bridge the gap between batteries and conventional capacitors, providing rapid charge/discharge for regenerative braking in electric vehicles

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Waves.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Waves applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: PET scans detect positron annihilation: the 1.022 MeV of mass-energy from an electron-positron pair converts to two 0.511 MeV gamma rays traveling in opposite directions
Ultrasound imaging: high-frequency sound waves reflect off internal structures; the time delay and intensity of reflected waves produce medical images of organs and fetuses
Hydraulic systems use Pascal's principle: a small force on a small piston creates a large force on a large piston (F₂ = F₁ · A₂/A₁), powering car brakes and industrial presses
Batteries maintain a fixed potential difference (EMF) between terminals; the chemical reaction supplies energy to move charges from low to high potential

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Waves.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Waves?

options.text: Doppler radar: police speed guns and weather radar measure the frequency shift of reflected radio waves to determine object velocity
Scuba diving: at depth, the increased pressure compresses air in the lungs (Boyle's law); divers must ascend slowly to avoid decompression sickness (the bends)
Thermal imaging cameras detect infrared radiation emitted by objects: used by firefighters to see through smoke and by engineers to find heat leaks in buildings
Seismology: P-waves (longitudinal) and S-waves (transverse) travel through Earth at different speeds, allowing scientists to locate earthquake epicenters and map Earth's interior structure

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Waves.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Waves in practice?

options.text: Scanning tunneling microscopes use quantum tunneling current between a sharp tip and a surface to image individual atoms on surfaces with sub-atomic resolution
Musical instruments: standing waves on strings and in air columns produce specific frequencies (harmonics); the length, tension, and density determine the pitch
Household wiring: outlets are wired in parallel (each at 120V) with circuit breakers in series (trip at 15-20A) to prevent overheating and fires
Atomic clocks use the precise frequency of electron transitions in cesium atoms (9.19 GHz) to define the second—accurate to 1 part in 10¹⁴

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Waves.
